Everyone needs to calm down. This is not the answer.

A bus and a house have been set ablaze in Belfast as hundreds of protesters responded to the brutal knife attack in the city last night - defying government ministers' calls for calm. There were ugly scenes on the streets, with masked men clashing with police, and reports of a group kicking in doors to 'get the foreigners out'. Fires were set across the city on vehicles, piles of rubble and streets barricades.

Who are the Inghams?

On the surface, the Inghams seem like any other attention-hungry British family documenting their lives on social media. Since starting their YouTube channel in 2015, parents Chris, 41, and Sarah, 40, have broadcast to their 1.38 million subscribers some of their most intimate moments, from footage of the birth of their son Jason to videos of their children opening presents on Christmas mornings and their move to Dubai. Their six children - Isabelle (whom Sarah had with a previous partner), Esme, Isla, Jason (known as Jace), Mila and Aurora, who range in age from two to 20 - are a permanent fixture in their content.

The Princess, who became the first member of the Royal family to compete in the Olympics in 1976, suffered a serious fall during the cross-country event. At the time, she was credited with refusing to let her team down and getting back on her horse, Goodwill, which was owned by her mother, Queen Elizabeth II. It’s nearly comical to me how people who have never grieved the death of someone who was the biggest part of their lives, seem to think a couple weeks or a couple months “dulls the pain” or they’ll be moving forward. I was a funeral director for years before my father died and I was a mess for the first year. Then fine for 6 months into the 2nd year, then a mess for the next 6 months, and now moving forward (he died in October 2023). The thing that surprised me the most is how come NOBODY ever told me how losing the most important parent in your life changes the world and changes you and you will just never be the same person again. I’m 50.

Rarely-seen pop star Sia steps out makeup-free in Los Angeles

Fans are used to seeing her dressed up with her face covered - usually by a large bow. But on Sunday, Australian pop star Sia swapped her trademark dramatics for a low-key look as she visited a farmers' market in Los Angeles. The singer, 50, looked relaxed in a cream kaftan layered over a white T-shirt as she perused the stalls and picked up some bags of vegetables.
